---
title: "Save & restore widget state"
---

Every cositos widget's state is plain data, so a whole UI — including composed,
nested widgets and their binary buffers — can be **saved to JSON and reconstructed
later**. cositos targets the stock ipywidgets *Widget State JSON schema v2*
(`application/vnd.jupyter.widget-state+json`), so saved files interoperate with the
wider ecosystem.

## Dump a document

`dump_document` takes `(model_id, state)` entries and returns the v2 envelope. Children
are referenced as `"IPY_MODEL_<id>"` strings, so composition needs nothing special.

```{python}
import json

from cositos import dump_document

entries = [
    ("box", {"_esm": "export default {}", "children": ["IPY_MODEL_slider"]}),
    ("slider", {"_esm": "export default {}", "value": 42}),
]
doc = dump_document(entries)
print(json.dumps(doc, indent=2))
```

Note the `version_major: 2 / version_minor: 0` envelope (the *state-format* version,
distinct from the protocol version `2.1.0`), and that the `box` keeps its
`"IPY_MODEL_slider"` reference verbatim.

## Restore it

`load_document` is the exact inverse — a flat, id-keyed pass, so reference cycles are
safe:

```{python}
from cositos import load_document

restored = load_document(doc)
assert restored == entries          # round-trips losslessly
restored
```

## Binary buffers

Binary values (e.g. a NumPy array's bytes) are split out and base64-encoded under a
`buffers` array; on load they are merged back. Buffers are compared by **raw bytes**, so a
typed `float32` view round-trips correctly:

```{python}
import array

arr = array.array("f", [1.5, 2.5, -3.0])
doc = dump_document([("plot", {"shape": [3], "dtype": "float32", "data": memoryview(arr)})])
doc["state"]["plot"]["buffers"]
```

That's the whole persistence model: `dump_document` / `load_document` (and the
single-widget `dump_model` / `load_model`). See also [static export](static-export.qmd)
to turn a saved document into a viewable HTML page.

## Download & restore a dashboard's state {#download-restore-a-dashboards-state}

The pattern above generalizes directly to a **real, running dashboard**:
[`examples/dashboard/build.py`](../../examples/dashboard/build.py)'s `Dashboard`
(`cositos-70b.4`) carries a `download_button.js` widget
([`examples/widgets/`](../../examples/widgets/), `cositos-70b.5`) whose `json` state
field is always `dump_document(...)` of the dashboard's current slider/dropdown/summary
state -- refreshed on every model change the exact same way the summary text is (see the
module's own docstring for why that MUST be through the shared host Model and never a
widget observing another widget). Clicking the button triggers a real browser download of
that JSON via a `data:` URI -- no server round-trip, no upload widget needed for the
*save* half.

**Restoring is docs-only in this pass** (deliberately: no new "upload" widget) -- exactly
the two functions above, called on whatever bytes your host's upload mechanism produces
(a Jupyter file-upload cell, `open(path).read()`, a browser `<input type="file">` handler
that hands the kernel a string, ...):

```python
from cositos import load_document
from examples.dashboard.build import Dashboard, restore_document

# The "save" half: what clicking the download button in a running dashboard produces.
dashboard = Dashboard(options=["low", "medium", "high"], value=42, index=2)
downloaded_json_text = dashboard._get_download_state()["json"]  # normally: a real file

# A FRESH session later: restore from the downloaded file's text.
restored_doc = restore_document(downloaded_json_text)   # == json.loads(...)
restored_entries = load_document(restored_doc)
by_id = dict(restored_entries)
by_id[dashboard.slider_id]["value"]      # 42 -- survives the round trip
by_id[dashboard.dropdown_id]["index"]    # 2
by_id[dashboard.summary_id]["text"]      # "value=42, selection=high"
```

Re-rendering the restored document (`embed_html(restored_doc, views=[...])`, per
[static export](static-export.qmd)) reproduces the same on-screen dashboard -- the same
real `@jupyter-widgets/controls` identity, the same values -- because `restored_doc` is a
plain cositos `Document` like any other; nothing about restore is dashboard-specific.
`tests/test_example_dashboard.py::test_restore_document_recovers_the_downloaded_state_in_a_fresh_session`
certifies this exact recipe against a real temp file, not just an in-memory string.

See [`tutorials/dashboard.qmd`](dashboard.qmd) for the same recipe walked through as a runnable, rendered tutorial (build → download → restore → confirm identical), rather than as isolated code snippets.
